title: Asteroid Blues
song: “Blues” — the genre, not a single song
episodeLink: The title weds the blues to the asteroid mining colony where the episode unfolds. Tonally it fits perfectly: the doomed lovers Asimov and Katerina end in a fatal shootout, carrying the melancholy, fatalistic ache that the blues — and the whole series — runs on. There is no single famous song called “Asteroid Blues”; it sets the emotional key for everything to follow.
songSignificance: The blues is the foundational African-American genre that gave rise to jazz, R&B, and rock and roll; its themes of hardship, longing, and loss underpin most of 20th-century popular music.

title: Stray Dog Strut
episodeLink: A play on the Stray Cats hit — “Cat” swapped for “Dog” because the episode is a chase for a genetically engineered Welsh Corgi (Ein, the Bebop's data dog). The strutting, prowling swagger of the original tune matches the episode's cool, playful tone.
songSignificance: “Stray Cat Strut” helped revive rockabilly in the 1980s, hitting the US top three in 1983; Brian Setzer's guitar work later ranked among Guitar World's greatest solos.

title: Honky Tonk Women
episodeLink: A direct nod to the Stones single. The episode introduces Faye Valentine as a hard-luck gambler and con artist working a casino — her swaggering, morally loose persona mirrors the song's portrait of worldly, hard-living women in barroom (honky-tonk) settings.
songSignificance: A UK No. 1 released the day after founder Brian Jones's death, it was the Stones' last UK chart-topper and the first single with Mick Taylor, built on Keith Richards' open-G riff.

title: Gateway Shuffle
song: “Shuffle” — a rhythmic feel, not a single song
episodeLink: The title pairs the “shuffle” groove with the hyperspace astral gates at the center of the plot, where eco-terrorists threaten Ganymede. It's wordplay on the gates plus a musical-style descriptor rather than a reference to one canonical track.
songSignificance: The shuffle rhythm is a backbone of blues, swing, and early rock and roll, defining the groove of countless standards in the jazz/blues idiom the series celebrates.

listen.label: Shuffle · e.g. Stevie Ray Vaughan — “Pride and Joy”
listen.query: Stevie Ray Vaughan Pride and Joy

title: Ballad of Fallen Angels
artist: Sometimes linked to Aerosmith's “Fallen Angels” — unconfirmed
episodeLink: This pivotal episode stages Spike's first confrontation with his former syndicate partner Vicious, whose line — “when angels are forced out of heaven, they become devils” — gives the title its meaning. “Ballad” frames the tragic, lyrical tone of Spike's fall with Julia. The exact musical referent is debated; it most likely invokes the ballad form itself.
songSignificance: As a form, the ballad is a narrative, emotionally driven song central to folk, jazz, and rock storytelling; the Aerosmith attribution is not firmly established.

title: Sympathy for the Devil
episodeLink: A direct reference to the Stones track. The episode follows Wen, a man trapped in a child's body who has lived over a century and become a cold killer — echoing the song's first-person devil who has “been around for a long, long year.” Ancient evil and ambiguous sympathy mirror the song's invitation to recognize evil within humanity.
songSignificance: The opening track of “Beggars Banquet” provoked rumors the Stones were devil worshippers, though its lyric actually indicts collective human culpability; it sits among Rolling Stone's 500 Greatest Songs.

listen.label: The Rolling Stones — “Sympathy for the Devil”
listen.query: Rolling Stones Sympathy for the Devil

title: Heavy Metal Queen
song: “Heavy metal” — the genre; the episode also nods to Deep Purple's “Space Truckin'” and C.W. McCall's “Convoy”
episodeLink: The title names trucker VT (Victoria Terpsichore), the “Heavy Metal Queen,” who blasts metal in her rig over a Yoko Kanno hard-rock cue. Its space-trucker setting and CB-radio culture nod to “Space Truckin'” and “Convoy,” but the title itself is genre flavor rather than one named song.
songSignificance: Heavy metal is a major rock subgenre born around 1970 (Black Sabbath, Deep Purple, Led Zeppelin), defined by amplified distortion and power; it became one of the most enduring rock styles worldwide.

title: Waltz for Venus
episodeLink: The title swaps “Debby” for “Venus,” the planet where the episode takes place, keeping the gentle waltz framing. The story of Rocco — a naive dreamer Spike reluctantly mentors, hoping to cure his blind sister — has the bittersweet, intimate quality of a slow jazz waltz, a small dreamer crushed by larger forces.
songSignificance: “Waltz for Debby” is Bill Evans' most famous composition and a cornerstone jazz standard; the live Village Vanguard recordings with bassist Scott LaFaro are considered some of the greatest piano-trio jazz ever made.

title: Jamming with Edward
artist: Nicky Hopkins, Ry Cooder & Rolling Stones members
episodeLink: The album was an impromptu, loose jam — which suits the episode where the crew chases an eccentric satellite hacker called “Edward,” who turns out to be the feral girl-genius Radical Edward. The title's improvisational spirit matches Ed's chaotic, free-form energy.
songSignificance: The record captured Stones members jamming during the “Let It Bleed” sessions while waiting for Keith Richards; “Edward” was Brian Jones's nickname for session pianist Nicky Hopkins.

listen.label: Nicky Hopkins & the Stones — “Jamming with Edward!”

title: Ganymede Elegy
song: “Elegy” — a mournful form, no specific song
episodeLink: Unusually, this title references no specific song; an “elegy” is a lament for the lost, paired with Ganymede, Jet's home moon. In his first spotlight, Jet reunites with his ex Alisa and must reckon with how she left him — finally throwing his broken pocket watch into the sea. A lament for a love that is gone.
songSignificance: As a form, the elegy is a mournful composition for the dead or the lost — kin to the slow jazz ballads that color the series, though no single song is referenced.

listen.label: Elegy · e.g. Miles Davis — “Blue in Green”

title: Toys in the Attic
episodeLink: References Aerosmith's album, where the phrase is also slang for being unhinged. The episode is a comedic horror riff on “Alien”: a mysterious creature loose in the Bebop's neglected storage spaces picks off the crew one by one — playing on both the literal junk-filled attic and the “losing your mind” connotation.
songSignificance: “Toys in the Attic” (1975) was Aerosmith's commercial breakthrough and best-selling US studio album, home to “Sweet Emotion” and “Walk This Way,” a landmark 1970s hard-rock record.

title: Jupiter Jazz (Part 1)
song: “Jazz” — the genre, planet Jupiter + the music
artist: A genre invocation (Mingus-style hard bop is a fan touchstone)
episodeLink: Pairs Jupiter's moon Callisto with “jazz,” fitting the smoky club mood at the Blue Crow where Faye meets the saxophone-playing ex-soldier Gren. Jazz is literally diegetic here — Gren plays sax, and the recurring “Julia” music-box melody threads through a noir tale of Spike, Vicious, and lost love.
songSignificance: Not a single song but the genre that defines the show's identity; Charles Mingus is often cited as a stylistic touchstone for this stretch of the series.

listen.label: Jazz · e.g. Charles Mingus — “Goodbye Pork Pie Hat”
listen.query: Charles Mingus Goodbye Pork Pie Hat

title: Jupiter Jazz (Part 2)
episodeLink: The conclusion resolves Gren — the saxophonist betrayed by Vicious — and reunites the threads of Julia, Spike, and the music box. A dying musician flying home to Titan, scored by Yoko Kanno's saxophone-led “Space Lion,” cements jazz as both the mood and the soul of the episode.
songSignificance: References the jazz genre central to the show; the arc is closely tied to Kanno's original composition “Space Lion,” one of the series' signature pieces.

title: Bohemian Rhapsody
episodeLink: A direct reference to Queen's epic. A reclusive chess grandmaster orchestrates a multi-part gate-toll heist using decoy bounty heads — a sprawling puzzle that mirrors the song's famously segmented, suite-like structure. Many disconnected pieces resolve into one composition, with a wistful, eccentric loner at its center.
songSignificance: From “A Night at the Opera,” “Bohemian Rhapsody” is one of the most innovative rock singles ever — a six-minute, operatic, multi-section track that topped the UK chart for nine weeks.

title: My Funny Valentine
artist: Rodgers & Hart (definitive: Chet Baker, Miles Davis)
episodeLink: A sentimental flashback into Faye Valentine's lost memories, revealing that “Valentine” isn't even her real name but a surname given by a doctor. The wistful, melancholy standard mirrors the bittersweet longing of Faye's backstory; the title puns the song against the character's name.
songSignificance: One of the most enduring American standards, recorded over 1,300 times; Chet Baker's vocal and Miles Davis's instrumental readings became cornerstones of the cool-jazz and hard-bop canon.

title: Black Dog Serenade
episodeLink: “Black Dog” doubles as Jet Black's old police nickname, and the episode digs into his dark past as a cop and his betrayal by a former partner (with a nod to the prison-transport thriller “Con Air”). The hard, brooding song pairs with the noir, vengeful tone; “Serenade” ironically softens the violence.
songSignificance: A signature track from “Led Zeppelin IV,” famous for its call-and-response a cappella phrasing and stuttering, off-kilter riff — one of the band's most recognizable songs.

title: Mushroom Samba
song: “Samba” + a hallucinogenic-mushroom trip; evokes '70s blaxploitation funk
artist: A genre/mood reference (Isaac Hayes, Curtis Mayfield-era soundtracks)
episodeLink: The crew eats hallucinogenic mushrooms and trips out while Ed hunts a bounty in a comedic homage to 1970s blaxploitation films (a “Coffy”-inspired hunter named Coffee, “Shaft” brothers). The upbeat samba/funk feel matches the trippy, retro-soundtrack energy, capped by Yoko Kanno's cue “Mushroom Hunting.”
songSignificance: Samba is the foundational popular dance music of Brazil and a cornerstone of Latin music that shaped bossa nova and jazz-funk; the blaxploitation soundtracks it evokes were landmark works of 1970s funk and soul.

title: Speak Like a Child
episodeLink: The episode hinges on a Betamax tape holding a childhood video message from a young Faye to her future self — a tender artifact confronting her jaded adult self. The album's dreamlike, wistful post-bop tone mirrors the episode's meditation on lost innocence and the gap between the child you were and the adult you became.
songSignificance: A landmark Blue Note album, Hancock's sixth as a leader, noted for lush, impressionistic arrangements (flugelhorn, bass trombone, alto flute) — a celebrated example of his lyrical post-bop writing.

listen.label: Herbie Hancock — “Speak Like a Child”
listen.query: Herbie Hancock Speak Like a Child

title: Wild Horses
episodeLink: Spike chases a bounty while a vintage Earth-built space-plane and an old mechanic devoted to obsolete machines anchor themes of nostalgia, freedom, and the things you can't quite let go of or tame. The world-weary ballad matches the reflective mood — “wild horses couldn't drag me away.”
songSignificance: A signature ballad from “Sticky Fingers,” one of the Stones' most beloved and widely covered songs, emblematic of their early-'70s country-rock turn.

title: Pierrot le Fou
song: Godard's 1965 film; villain names lifted from Yellow Magic Orchestra's “Mad Pierrot” & “Tong Poo”
artist: Jean-Luc Godard (film); Yellow Magic Orchestra (the names)
episodeLink: A childlike, deranged, near-invincible assassin called Mad Pierrot stalks Spike — the menacing “sad clown” archetype (the Japanese title means “Requiem for a Clown”). Godard's stylish, melancholic New Wave gangster cinema shaped the whole series, and the villain's and handler's names (“Mad Pierrot,” “Tongpu”) come straight from YMO songs.
songSignificance: Godard's “Pierrot le Fou” is a canonical French New Wave masterpiece; YMO were pioneers of electronic and synth-pop whose influence on later electronica is immense.

title: Boogie Woogie Feng Shui
artist: Pinetop Smith, Meade Lux Lewis, Albert Ammons
episodeLink: The title yokes a Western genre (boogie-woogie) to an Eastern divination practice (feng shui), mirroring the plot in which Jet teams up with Meifa, who uses feng shui to track down her father Pao. A playful East-meets-West juxtaposition that fits the episode's lighter, whimsical tone.
songSignificance: Boogie-woogie is a fast, rhythmic blues-piano style from early-20th-century African-American communities that became a craze in the late 1930s-40s and heavily shaped rhythm and blues and rock and roll.

title: Cowboy Funk
artist: James Brown, Parliament-Funkadelic, Sly & the Family Stone
episodeLink: Fuses the show's own cowboy/bounty-hunter motif with funk in a comedic Western pastiche built around rival hunter “Cowboy Andy” and the eco-terrorist Teddy Bomber. The “funk” is tonal swagger and genre flavor more than a single song — a loose, self-parodying groove.
songSignificance: Funk is a rhythm-driven African-American genre of syncopated bass and percussion, pioneered by James Brown and expanded by Parliament-Funkadelic and Sly & the Family Stone; it became foundational to disco and hip-hop.

title: Brain Scratch
episodeLink: Widely read as a play on “scratching” a record, tied to the cult SCRATCH, whose leader Dr. Londes lures followers to abandon their bodies for an “infinite sea of electrons” via television. The disruptive “scratch” metaphor matches the cult's destabilizing, mind-warping message; whether it points to one song is uncertain.
songSignificance: Scratching is a turntablism technique developed by 1970s New York hip-hop DJs (often credited to Grand Wizzard Theodore), moving a record under the needle to make rhythmic sound — a defining element of hip-hop and electronic music.

title: Hard Luck Woman
episodeLink: Centered on Faye and Ed and their search for identity and belonging: Faye recovers a clue to her erased past, and Ed reunites with her father before she and Ein leave the Bebop. The “hard luck woman” fits Faye — an amnesiac drifter dogged by bad fortune — lending a bittersweet, wistful mood as the crew quietly fractures.
songSignificance: A Top 20 US hit from KISS's 1976 “Rock and Roll Over,” written by Paul Stanley and sung by drummer Peter Criss; originally conceived for Rod Stewart, it became one of KISS's signature soft-rock-tinged ballads.

title: The Real Folk Blues (Part 1)
artist: Chess Records blues series; Bebop's ending theme by Mai Yamane & Yoko Kanno
episodeLink: The two-part finale plunges into Spike's tragic past, reuniting him with Julia against Vicious and the Red Dragon Syndicate in a mournful, fatalistic blues key. The title doubles as the show's recurring ending theme, sung by Mai Yamane, whose lyric — “Aren't the real emotions only found in dreams?” — crystallizes the series' sense of loss.
songSignificance: “The Real Folk Blues” was a landmark Chess Records compilation series (1965-67) that repackaged electric blues by Muddy Waters and Howlin' Wolf for the 1960s blues-revival audience — among the finest gateways to Chicago blues.

listen.label: The Seatbelts — “The Real Folk Blues”
listen.query: Cowboy Bebop The Real Folk Blues Mai Yamane

title: The Real Folk Blues (Part 2)
artist: Chess Records blues series; Bebop's ending theme by Mai Yamane & Yoko Kanno
episodeLink: The series finale resolves Spike's confrontation with Vicious and his grief over Julia in a somber, blues-soaked climax (it closes not on “The Real Folk Blues” but on “Blue”). The title underscores fate, mortality, and a past that cannot be outrun as Spike walks knowingly to his end — “Bang.”
songSignificance: The Chess “Real Folk Blues” series (1965-67) repackaged electric Chicago blues for the folk/blues-revival audience and remains one of the strongest compilation runs in the Chess catalog — a key gateway to Muddy Waters and Howlin' Wolf.
